question: explain fitting and validation from the following pdf
answer:
Fitting refers to the process of training a model on a dataset. Underfitting occurs when a model is too simple and cannot capture the underlying trend of the data, resulting in high bias and low variance. Overfitting occurs when a model is too complex and captures the noise of the data, resulting in low bias and high variance.

Validation is the process of evaluating the performance of a model on a separate dataset. Cross-validation is a technique used to reduce variability in the validation results. It involves partitioning the data into training and testing sets, training the model on the training set, and evaluating its performance on the testing set. This process is repeated multiple times with different partitions of the data, and the results are combined to give an estimate of the model's predictive performance.

Types of cross-validation include Leave-One-Out Cross Validation (LOOCV), Leave-P-Out cross Validation (LOOPV), Holdout Method, K-Fold Cross Validation, and Stratified K-Fold Cross Validation. Each method has its own strengths and weaknesses, such as LOOCV being computationally expensive and Holdout Method being simple but potentially unstable. K-Fold Cross Validation is a method that divides the dataset into k subsets and repeats the holdout method k times to guarantee that the score of the model does not depend on the way the train and test sets are picked.
